perturbation.original_text: (e) No action, regardless of form, related to, or arising out of this Agreement may be brought by either party more than two (2) years after the cause of action has arisen.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) No action, regardless of form, related to, or arising out of this Agreement may be brought by either party more than one (1) year after the cause of action has arisen.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the statute of limitations to one year may conflict with Indiana's statute of limitations for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana Code § 34-11-2-7 establishes a six-year statute of limitations for actions relating to contracts that are not in writing. Since the contract does not explicitly state in writing a shorter statute of limitations, a contract cannot impose such a statute of limitations below the period in the statute. Reducing it to one year directly contradicts this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the statute of limitations for contract-related actions from two years to one year. The Law Explanation states that Indiana Code § 34-11-2-7 establishes a six-year statute of limitations for non-written contracts, implying a minimum standard. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is inaccessible, the Law Explanation suggests the one-year limitation could be considered a contradiction of established limitations. Because the original text of the contract already stated a shorter limitation period of two years, the changed text that reduces the limitation to one year would still be considered a contradiction in that it violates federal or state law. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: (d) Customer, upon thirty days prior written notice, may cancel this Agreement at the end of the Initial Term and thereafter on each anniversary of the end of the Initial Term.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) Customer, upon fifteen days prior written notice, may cancel this Agreement at the end of the Initial Term and thereafter on each anniversary of the end of the Initial Term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cancellation notice to 15 days might be deemed unreasonable and unconscionable, especially if it doesn't afford adequate time to find alternative service.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires reasonable notification before a contract is terminated, especially if its termination would be detrimental to the other party. While this agreement isn't strictly a sale of goods, Indiana courts often apply UCC principles to service contracts. Fifteen days' notice may be deemed unreasonable if the customer relies heavily on the service, contradicting UCC 2-309's principle of adequate notification and opportunity to seek alternatives. This creates a potential conflict, especially when considering the reliance and dependence on the technical infrastructure maintenance provided.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notification period for contract cancellation from 30 days to 15 days. The LLM explanation and the perturbation explanation both suggest this could be unreasonable, potentially violating the UCC's requirement for 'reasonable notification' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically § 2-309(3). The modified clause may not afford the other party enough time to adjust, creating a contradiction of the legal standard.
